data.title: Camping World: Shares Aren't Cheap Enough To Account For What's To Come
data.description:
One company that I have known about for a long time but have not yet written about is Camping World Holdings, Inc. (CWH). At long last, I decided to tackle the firm. In recent years, financial performance has come under pressure. But lately, we have started to see something of a turnaround from a revenue perspective. And while net profits are still on the decline, cash flows are growing. Management has he even provided some guidance for the 2026 fiscal year, pointing toward a continued return to growth.

As great as this is, however, I doubt that this makes for a great prospect for long-term investors. Operationally, I suspect that the business will do fine. But there are certain industry challenges that it has to contend with, and the valuation of the business is not as cheap as I would like it to be. Because of this, I think that rating it a "Hold" as opposed to something more optimistic is the right choice.

Taking Camping World Holdings On A Test Drive

In case you are new to Camping World Holdings, the company operates as one of the largest RV retailers on the planet. As of this writing, the institution has 197 locations, with those split between its Camping World and Good Sam brands. In fact, I have a tiny tie to the company myself. Years ago, my parents purchased their Coachmen Leprechaun RV from one of the firm’s Camping World locations. And they still have that RV to this day. In fact, when weather permits, going camping in it is one of their favorite hobbies. But I digress. In addition to this, the company sells related products and services. These include, but are not limited to, roadside assistance plans, protection plans and insurance, and even maintenance and repair work.

I wish I could say that the past few years have been a good time for the RV space. But honestly, it hasn't been. Thanks to government stimulus and the desire to engage in social distancing during the COVID-19 pandemic, there was a surge in buying activity in the RV market. For instance, while in 2019 wholesale shipments in the space amounted to just 406,070 units, by 2021, that number had ballooned to 600,240. This spike was essentially front-loading future years of demand. But since then, we have seen a decline. RV shipments bottomed out at 313,174 in 2023. In 2024, they inched up slightly to 333,733. However, even at that level, other than the year before, this was the lowest number of shipments that the industry had seen since 2013.

This year, RV sales are forecasted to come in at between 320,400 and 353,500. At the midpoint, this would represent only a 1% increase over what was seen last year. And next year, we are looking at between 332,400 and 366,100, with the midpoint of 329,000 representing a 3.6% rise year over year. My own personal opinion is that RV Sales next year will probably be lower. As I have detailed in other articles, the U.S. economy seems to be barreling toward a recession. Yes, interest rates are declining, and that should certainly help to spur some RV purchases. But the fact of the matter is that consumer confidence is getting weaker, and tariffs should play a deleterious role in consumer demand, especially at a time when the jobs market is looking the weakest that it has since the pandemic.

It's not surprising, then, that as the chart above illustrates, financial performance for the business took a hit over the last three years. Revenue plunged from $6.97 billion to $6.10 billion. And while cash flows were a bit lumpier, the overall trend for those and for net profits was negative. This weakness came from tough market conditions. But interestingly, the overall number of units sold by the company did not contract all that much.

Yes, there was a plunge from 70,429 new units sold in 2022 to 58,731 units sold in 2023. But this was offset to some extent by a rise in the number of used units sold from 51,325 to 56,823. In short, as industry conditions worsened, customers traded down, eschewing the high-cost and significant depreciation that new vehicles experience in favor of used units that should preserve their value better. But then, in 2024, as the number of new units rebounded to 70,484, the number of used units dropped to 51,032.

The overall decline in revenue then was driven in large part by a major contraction in the average price of a unit sold. This makes sense given the state of the industry. Revenue per unit for new vehicles dropped from $45,834 to $40,089. Meanwhile, used units saw a contraction in price from $36,583 to $31,624. These changes had a meaningful impact on the company’s gross profit per unit, as the chart above illustrates. And that is what actively affected bottom-line results disproportionately relative to the decline in revenue that the company exhibited.

The only saving grace here was that finance and insurance revenue, which is essentially all profit, held up quite well. And while there was a decline in the average gross profit per unit on this front, that drop was modest, from $5,121 to $4,935. Anybody who has analyzed many companies in the automotive retail space can tell you that it's the finance and insurance side of the company, and sometimes to a lesser extent the service side, that accounts for a lot of the profitability, even though revenue is a fraction of the overall pie. So it's not shocking to see that here as well.

For this year, we are seeing some nice improvements. A rise in the number of new units sold from 58,909 in the first nine months of 2024 to 63,708 units at the same time this year, as well as a rise in the number of used units sold from 40,459 to 51,539, more than offset the declines in prices experienced on a per vehicle basis. As a result, revenue for this period grew from $4.90 billion to $5.20 billion. This came at a time when management even reduced the number of locations in operation from 207 to 197 as it seeks to cut costs and operate more efficiently. Net profits, as the chart above illustrates, are still down, even though finance and insurance revenue grew nicely. But cash flows for the company are largely improving, with adjusted operating cash flow nearly doubling and EBITDA shooting up.

Management does seem optimistic. Next year, for instance, management is forecasting EBITDA of around $310 million as the company pushes forward to grow its market share to 20% and as management continues to push forward on cost-cutting initiatives, especially in light of major uncertainty regarding the tariff environment. Hitting that target for profitability would likely mean adjusted operating cash flow of around $159.5 million. And for this year, we are probably looking at EBITDA and adjusted operating cash flow of $265.4 million and $136.6 million, respectively.

Using these estimates, and also using the historical results that the company achieved for last year, we can see in the chart above how shares of the company are currently priced. These are multiples that aren't bad, but they aren't cheap enough in my opinion to justify upside. Relative to other similar firms, the stock is not exactly cheap either. In the table below, I compared it to five such enterprises. And what I found was that, on a price to operating cash flow basis, two of the firms are cheaper than it is, while another is tied with it. But because of the high net debt that the business has of $2.75 billion, its EV to EBITDA multiple puts it as the second highest among this group.

Company Price / Operating Cash Flow EV / EBITDA
Camping World Holdings 9.5 15.9
THOR Industries (THO) 9.5 10.9
Winnebago Industries (WGO) 8.2 13.3
LCI Industries (LCII) 6.8 9.1
Lazydays Holdings (GORV) 31.2 59.6
Patrick Industries (PATK) 9.8 11.1

Takeaway

Based on the data provided, I believe that while the worst is probably over for Camping World Holdings, there is likely some additional weakness around the corner. I anticipate that RV sales will be lower next year than what has been anticipated. If the stock were cheaper, I would be more optimistic. But given where things stand currently, I believe a "Hold" rating is appropriate.

data.title: Conagra: High-Yield Dividend Trap Or Undervalued Opportunity?
data.description:
Intro

When investors look at Conagra Brands, Inc. (CAG), their first impressions are likely: “What a great dividend yield,” likely followed by, “Why has the stock dropped so much?” The stock chart certainly paints quite a scary classic falling knife scenario. Still, I’m trying to look past the natural hesitation here and unpack the underlying investment case — to assess whether the market may have overreacted with the sell-off and whether a compelling opportunity might be found as a result.

Conagra 3-year chart:

In my experience, the food industry tends to be a kind of slow mover, driven by sticky long-term trends. Turnarounds are usually lengthy, and shifts take time. That’s why I pay special attention to Conagra dividends — whether they could provide attractive, financially well-backed income while the company regains its footing, or if they’re at risk of being cut.

Having done this analysis, I rate Conagra as a Buy at these levels. That said, it’s a cautious buy, and investors should consider phasing in their positions gradually, adjusting both quantity and timing. My rating applies while the stock remains in the $17–18 range — or below, of course — as the risks are still quite high.

I believe management must execute at a very high level to keep pace with changing consumer habits. While Conagra’s path to growth may be difficult to envision in the near term, maintaining a relatively stable trajectory and delivering an attractive dividend seems achievable. In short, I don’t see strong capital gain potential here, but for high-yield dividend seekers, it’s a compelling case — one of the safest dividends I’ve seen in a while among high yielders, in the 7–8% range.

Conagra in Brief

This is how the company presents itself:

Conagra Brands, Inc. is a U.S.-based holding company in the consumer packaged goods sector, with its headquarters in Chicago, Illinois. The company produces and markets a wide range of branded food products found in grocery stores, restaurants, and foodservice channels.

The Conagra Story

Conagra traces its origins to 1919, when Alva Kinney founded Nebraska Consolidated Mills. It was rebranded as ConAgra in 1971 and went public in 1973. Over the next two decades, the company expanded aggressively, acquiring around 200 businesses — including major deals like Monfort (1990), Lamb Weston, and Beatrice Foods (1987) — making it the world’s largest meatpacker and the second-largest food processor at the time. In 2000, the Company was rebranded as ConAgra Foods. Following a major restructuring in 2016, which also included cutting 1,500 jobs and relocating its headquarters to Chicago, the company rebranded again as Conagra Brands. Its most significant recent acquisition was Pinnacle Foods in 2018, for $8.1 billion.

A more detailed look at their full story can be found here.

The revenue breakdown as of 2025 (the Conagra fiscal year is from August to August)

Conagra's largest customer is Walmart (WMT) and its affiliates, which accounted for approximately 29% of consolidated net sales for fiscal 2025 and 28% for fiscal 2024 and 2023.

The current management team consists of long-time company veterans: Sean M. Connolly has served as President and CEO since 2015, David S. Marberger has been Executive Vice President and CFO since 2016, and Thomas M. McGough has held the role of Executive Vice President and COO since 2013.

Recent Developments

Fiscal Q1 2026 Earnings Highlights:

  • Non-GAAP EPS: $0.39, beat estimates by $0.06.
  • Revenue: $2.63 billion, down 5.7% year-over-year, was a hair $10 million above expectations.

Q1 Performance trends across segments:

Management summarized the quarter’s dynamics:

Sales trends, though still negative, showed improvement compared to the previous quarter:

Some insights from the best and worst sellers, protein-based products continue to show strong performance:

Despite solid customer demand for these, protein-based products continued to pressure margins, according to management’s comments on the earnings call:

"Proteins remain the largest headwind as we continue to see double-digit inflation in areas such as beef, pork, chicken, turkey and eggs."

Outlook

Management provided FY2026 outlook:

J.P. Morgan U.S. Opportunities Forum

The most recent management view comes from their November 12 presentation at the JP Morgan Conference, where both CEO Sean Connolly and CFO David Marberger participated. I found their remarks about current sales particularly insightful.

I think for the past 4 weeks, our volumes were off 1.5 points. So it's not as if we're a country mile off of our -- the midpoint of our long-term algorithm, but we're off and we're off because consumers are making some of these behavior shifts in response to that massive amount of cumulative inflation.

From what I gather, the focus is on volumes, so this shouldn’t be taken as an indication that they’re falling short of their FY organic growth guidance of –1% to +1%, which is expressed in dollar terms.

The CEO also discussed cautious consumer behavior, with shoppers staying within tighter budgets and trading down on everyday purchases. However, he remains confident that this is a transitional phase and that consumers will return to their preferred tastes and choices over time.

Another valuable summary, in my opinion, came again from the CEO. Considering that Conagra is one of the largest players in the industry worldwide, with a highly diversified portfolio that gives the CEO strong insight into real-life market dynamics.

I look at what's going on right now with less alcohol consumption, less sugar consumption, less carb consumption. There seems to be just more of an overall focus on health and wellness, particularly among younger consumers, that could prove to be a stickier behavior.

He followed up by highlighting Conagra’s relatively stronger position compared to companies with more sugar- and carb-oriented portfolios:

So fortunately, we don't compete in a lot of those categories. But when I do see something like that, it makes me think of manufacturers needing to change their innovation priorities and tailor them to what consumers are looking for today, which after all, frankly, is what we do in the consumer packaged goods business anyway. We're constantly responding to changing consumer desires and tastes.

Management also outlined their frozen food strategy and emphasized the segment’s strengths, noting how well it aligns with current consumer trends.

It is fresh food that it can be incredibly healthy for you incredibly clean label, loaded with protein, loaded with vegetable nutrition. And then we flash freeze it at the peak of freshness.

The CEO expressed his view on protein in general, calling it

"... is on fire right now. That shows up in snacks. It shows up across all different types of protein, from plant-based to animal-based. But within the animal-based protein world, chicken is absolutely on fire."

Additionally, CEO Connolly highlighted a major consumer shift in snacking toward protein, which also benefits Conagra’s seeds business. Their strategy is focused on boosting volume strength in snacks and frozen foods, while concentrating on cash generation in other areas like canned goods.

The CFO projected an additional $700 million in debt reduction this year, expressed confidence in maintaining the dividend, and confirmed no opportunistic share repurchases. Management expects profit growth ahead, noting that this year’s EPS guidance is down $0.25 due to net inflation. As animal protein costs decline, they don’t plan price cuts, allowing margin recovery and improved cash flow.

Their strategic approach across segments was effectively summarized:

I think going forward, what you should expect from our company is that every 5 years, a meaningfully larger chunk of our total portfolio sales will be focused in frozen and snacks, and that means that our grocery business will decline.

Dividend Sustainability

For me, the first thing to look at when assessing dividend sustainability is definitely the cash flow statement, followed by indebtedness metrics and overall liquidity. In addition, I pay close attention to management’s comments on capital allocation plans, as analysts frequently question the sustainability of dividends in the case of high-yielding stocks. Dividend history is also an important factor to consider— a long-standing tradition is not something management would disrupt lightly.

Last quarter’s cash flow was somewhat alarming, as it showed a negative result. Net debt has trended down, but due to falling profitability, net leverage decreased only marginally.

The cash flow outlook is crucial when evaluating whether future dividend payments are covered. Management provided the following cash flow and net debt dynamics for the remainder of FY26:

In my view, it’s also important to review debt covenants, as they could restrict dividend payments if financial results weaken. In case management’s estimates hold, everything appears to be fine from that perspective, given the projected multiple of 3.85 for FY2026, while the covenant still allows for some further weakening up to a multiple of 4.5.

Important information from the Company's latest SEC filing regarding liquidity in the case of a downgrade of the credit rating.

As of the end of the first quarter of fiscal 2026, our senior long-term debt ratings were all investment grade. A significant downgrade in our credit ratings would not affect our ability to borrow amounts under the Revolving Credit Facility, although borrowing costs would increase. A downgrade of our short-term credit ratings would impact our ability to borrow under our commercial paper program by negatively impacting borrowing costs and causing shorter durations, as well as making access to commercial paper more difficult, or impossible

In addition, all three major rating agencies — Fitch, S&P, and Moody’s — have affirmed ratings at BBB-/Baa3 with a stable outlook.

I also look at the company’s dividend history when assessing whether its dividend payments might be at risk. Everything appears solid on that front:

Management further confirmed their plans to maintain the current dividend level. The CFO spoke during the earnings call: "We're also returning capital to shareholders as we expect to maintain our $1.40 annual dividend rate."

The Q1 presentation illustrates the company’s capital allocation projections:

Considering the weak cash flow performance in the latest quarter, the CFO provided the following remarks: "..this is just solely timing, we built more inventory in the first quarter because remember, we were coming off supply disruptions."

Valuation

Taking into account Conagra’s relatively conservative FY2026 adjusted EBITDA projection of $1.8 billion, the EV/EBITDA multiple stands at 8.8. This compares to its historical average of 10.8 and peer valuations — Campbell's (CPB) at 9.5, General Mills (GIS) at 10.2, Hormel Foods (HRL) at 11.2, and Kraft Heinz (KHC) at 8.3, though the latter may warrant a lower multiple due to its own unique challenges. Based on this, I believe Conagra is currently conservatively priced. That said, I don’t want to underestimate the impact of a nominal negative ~5% growth rate and the broader secular shift in consumer behavior, which at least partially justifies the current low share price.

Share Price based on EV/EBITDA calculation, billions $
EBITDA fwd 1.8
Cash 0.7
Debt 8.2
Net Debt 7.6
EV/EBITDA 8.8
Equity Value/Mcap 8.4
Stocks 0.48
Stock Price 17.5

Risks

I think management did a great job of clearly summarizing the key risks during their latest earnings call:

Additionally, I think execution risk is certainly worth attention, as the ongoing changes in the marketplace present significant challenges that need to be addressed by management very effectively.

Takeaway

To me, Conagra appears well-positioned to stabilize its business — management holds all the right cards to stop the bleeding and potentially return to a modest long-term growth trajectory. The consumer packaged foods market, which had already been gradually shifting toward healthier options, is now accelerating in that direction, boosted by the rising popularity of GLP-1 weight-loss drugs. Conagra seems to have recognized this new reality — one defined by healthier choices and smaller portions — and is taking steps to adapt accordingly.

If the company can continue aligning with these trends — and its scale warrants some patience — then, despite the market’s rather skeptical view at the moment, I believe Conagra deserves a place in the value-oriented or dividend-harvesting part of an investor’s portfolio.

data.title: One High-Yield Stock (5.77%) And One Dividend Growth Pick (10.35% CAGR) For Our Dividend Portfolio
data.description:
The acquisition of additional shares of Microsoft (NASDAQ:MSFT) and VICI Properties (NYSE:VICI) helps improve our dividend portfolio's balance of income and dividend growth potential while increasing the proportion of our portfolio that is allocated to the Information Technology and the Real Estate Sector.

While VICI Properties pays investors currently a Dividend Yield [FWD] of 5.77%, Microsoft has strong dividend growth potential, underlined by its 10-Year Dividend Growth Rate [CAGR] of 10.35%.

Through our latest portfolio additions, we have increased the proportion that is allocated to Microsoft from 1.14% to 2.58% and to VICI Properties from 0.83% to 2.38%.

The proportion that is allocated to the Information Technology Sector has been increased from 5.76% to 7.25% and the proportion allocated to the Real Estate Sector has been raised from 8.10% to 9.53%.

After our latest additions, the Weighted Average Dividend Yield [TTM] and 5-Year Weighted Average Dividend Growth Rate [CAGR] stand at 4.14% and 7.46%, respectively.

Why I added additional shares of Microsoft to The Dividend Income Accelerator Portfolio

As I explained in greater detail in my previous article on Seeking Alpha, I suggest overweighting both Alphabet and Microsoft in a diversified dividend portfolio that focuses on dividend growth.

With a proportion of 3.34% on the overall portfolio, Alphabet is already among the three largest positions of The Dividend Income Accelerator Portfolio.

Through the acquisition of additional shares of Microsoft to our dividend portfolio, we have increased the company's proportion from 1.14% to 2.58%.

Microsoft strongly aligns with the investment approach of our dividend portfolio due to the company's competitive edge over competitors, its strong dividend growth potential, and its attractive risk-reward profile.

Microsoft in terms of Valuation

Microsoft currently has a P/E [FWD] Ratio of 32.41. This means that Microsoft's current P/E [FWD] Ratio is 4.41% above the Sector Median and 1.76% above the company's 5-Year Average, indicating a fair Valuation.

However, from my point of view, Microsoft deserves a Premium Valuation, given the company's significant competitive advantages, the company's broad product portfolio, and its high growth rates (EBIT Growth Rate [YoY] of 17.45%).

Microsoft in terms of Profitability

Microsoft's A+ rating in terms of Profitability Grade is underlined by the company's Return on Equity [TTM] of 33.28%, which is significantly above the Sector Median of 5.42%, its Return on Total Capital of 19.55%, which is well above the Sector Median of 3.81%, and its Gross Profit Margin [TTM] of 68.82%, which stands significantly above the Sector Median of 49.65%.

Microsoft's strong dividend growth potential

Microsoft currently has a Dividend Payout Ratio [FY1] [Non GAAP] of 22.56%, which serves as an indicator of the company's strong dividend growth potential. This is also underlined by Microsoft's 10-Year Dividend Growth Rate [CAGR] of 10.35%.

Additionally, it can be mentioned Microsoft's EPS Diluted Growth Rate [FWD] of 15.55%, which is another indicator of Microsoft's potential to raise the dividend in the future.

Furthermore, it can be highlighted Microsoft's EBIT Growth Rate [FWD] of 15.72%, which further strengthens my belief that Microsoft can strongly contribute to our dividend portfolio's future dividend growth potential.

Why I added additional shares of VICI Properties to The Dividend Income Accelerator Portfolio

Through the acquisition of additional shares of VICI Properties for The Dividend Income Accelerator Portfolio, we have increased the company's proportion on the overall portfolio from 0.83% to 2.38%.

With a current Dividend Yield [FWD] of 5.77% and the company's 5-Year Dividend Growth Rate [CAGR] of 7.41%, VICI Properties not only offers investors attractive dividend income potential, but also significant potential to increase this dividend income year over year.

VICI Properties' Dividend Income Potential

Below you can find Consensus Dividend Estimates for VICI Properties. For 2025, the Consensus Yield stands at 5.66%, for 2026 at 5.83%, and for 2027, at 6.00%, underlining my theory that VICI Properties can be an adequate investment choice for both investors looking for dividend income and for dividend growth.

The largest positions of The Dividend Income Accelerator Portfolio after adding Microsoft and VICI Properties

Berkshire Hathaway

After adding additional shares of Microsoft and VICI Properties to our dividend portfolio, the portfolio proportion that is allocated to Berkshire Hathaway (NYSE:BRK.A)(NYSE:BRK.B) has decreased from previously 4.98% to now 4.96%. Despite the fact that Berkshire Hathaway does not pay dividends to its shareholders, the company collects large amounts of dividends and strongly aligns with the investment approach of our investment portfolio due to its focus on companies with significant competitive advantages that are financially healthy.

Alphabet

Alphabet's (NASDAQ:GOOG) (NASDAQ:GOOGL) proportion of the overall portfolio has increased to 3.34%. This means that Alphabet is presently the second-largest position of our dividend portfolio. Like Microsoft, Alphabet will significantly contribute to our portfolio's dividend growth potential.

Ares Capital

Through the acquisition of additional shares of Microsoft and VICI Properties, the proportion of our investment portfolio that is allocated to Ares Capital (NASDAQ:ARCC) has decreased from 3.26% to 3.22%. This means that Ares Capital remains the third-largest position of our dividend portfolio.

BlackRock

After adding shares of Microsoft and VICI Properties to our dividend portfolio, the portfolio's proportion that is allocated to BlackRock (NYSE:BLK) has decreased from 3.09% to 2.89%, implying that BlackRock is presently the fourth largest position of our overall portfolio.

Microsoft

After adding additional shares of Microsoft to our dividend portfolio, we have increased the company's proportion compared to the overall portfolio from 1.14% to 2.58%. By overweighting Microsoft within our portfolio, we optimize the portfolio's risk-reward profile and strengthen its dividend growth potential.

Conclusion

By adding additional shares of Microsoft and VICI Properties to our dividend portfolio, we have not only optimized our portfolio's risk-reward profile, but also kept our portfolio balance of income and dividend growth while, simultaneously, increasing the proportion allocated to the Information Technology and the Real Estate Sector.

The portfolio proportion allocated to the Information Technology Sector has increased from 5.76% to 7.25% and the proportion allocated to the Real Estate Sector has increased from 8.10% to 9.53%. This demonstrates that we have increased our portfolio's level of sector diversification, thereby reducing our portfolio's risk level and improving our portfolio's risk-reward profile.

Our portfolio's Weighted Average Dividend Yield [TTM] now stands at 4.14% while its 5-Year Weighted Average Dividend Growth Rate [CAGR] is at 7.46%, indicating that our portfolio continues to balance income, dividend growth, and capital appreciation, allowing investors to invest with a reduced risk-level while generating substantial dividend income.

data.title: CRISPR Therapeutics Post Q3 Earnings: Testing Investor Patience, But Still A Buy
data.description:
Investment Overview

Zug, Switzerland-based CRISPR Therapeutics AG (CRSP) announced its Q3 earnings this Monday, 10th November. The gene therapy company revealed an R&D spend for the quarter of $58.9m, down from $82.2m in the prior year period, and collaboration expense - costs shared with partner Vertex (VRTX) for the development and commercialization of Casgevy, the approved ex-vivo Sickle Cell Disease and transfusion dependent Beta Thalassemia ("TDT") gene therapy - of $57.1m. Net loss amounted to $(106.4m), compared to $(85.9m) in the prior year.

On a nine-month basis, R&D costs amounted to $201m, compared to $238.5m in the prior year period, collaboration expense $160m, up from $110.3m, and loss from operations was reported as $(509.8m), compared to $(402m) in 2024.

Where Is The Casgevy Windfall?

CRISPR Therapeutics is arguably best known for being the first company - alongside partner Vertex - to secure approval for a CRISPR gene editing therapy. In 2021, when Vertex paid CRISPR Therapeutics $900m to increase its share of Casgevy revenues and profits from 50%, to 60%, CRISPR Therapeutics stock reached its all-time high value of ~$200 per share, but as we can see below, despite the approval of Casgevy in December 2023, shares have never come close to recapturing those highs:

On numerous occasions, as we can see, the stock has built some momentum, only to correct downwards again. Since I posted a note to Seeking Alpha in mid-September entitled "why the stock fell on Q2 earnings, and why we're now back on track," shares initially climbed from $60 - $78 per share, but then came another slide, and today they have sunk back to $52 per share pre-market.

CRISPR Therapeutics is not all about Casgevy - as we'll discuss, the company has four "therapeutic franchises" - heme, i.e., Casgevy, CAR-T - allogeneic cell therapies targeting oncology and autoimmune diseases, in vivo - phase 1 programs targeting cardiovascular disease, cholesterol, and liver disease, and an allogeneic beta-cell replacement therapy for diabetes.

Nevertheless, the promise of Casgevy, which pivotal studies show can keep patients free from painful vaso-occlusive crises (VOCs) for years at a time, and keep TDT patients transfusion free for years at a time, has always been a major contributor to CRISPR Therapeutics valuation.

Investors are entitled to therefore wonder why, nearly two years on from the approval of Casgevy, the company still reports no revenues for its sole approved drug?

In my last note I explained that - being a cell therapy that works by extracting a patients' bone marrow cells, transporting them to a lab and engineering them (in the case of Casgevy to express fetal hemoglobin, increasing production of healthy red blood cells), and then reinfusing them back into the patient, the overall treatment process can take up to a year.

Casgevy is approved in the U.S., Great Britain, the EU, the Kingdom of Saudi Arabia ("KSA"), the Kingdom of Bahrain (Bahrain), Qatar, Canada, Switzerland, and the United Arab Emirates ("UAE") - a total addressable market ("TAM") of 60k, CRISPR Therapeutics believes.

Based on a Casgevy's ~$2.2m list price, the TAM is ~$132bn dollars, but many patients may not be able to afford treatment, many may be sceptical / fearful of a long treatment process which could cause infertility, many may not be aware such a treatment exists, or prefer to continue with existing standards of care e.g. oral hydroxyurea.

CRISPR Therapeutics does reveal in its Q3 earnings press release, however, that:

Globally, since launch through September 30th, 2025, approximately 165 patients with SCD or TDT have completed their first cell collection, including 50 people in the third quarter. 39 patients have received infusions of CASGEVY, including 10 infused in the third quarter of 2025. Nearly 300 patients have been referred by their physicians to an authorized treatment center (ATC) to begin the treatment process. This includes 110 cell collections in the first nine months of 2025, double the total for all of 2024.

Vertex stated the same in its Q3 earnings release, although it also added that it had recorded $16.9m and $61.5m of Casgevy revenues for Q3 and year-to-date, respectively. That's likely not the revenue figure that Vertex had in mind when it paid $900m to CRISPR Therapeutics back in 2021 - but could that trickle of revenues turn into a flood in time?

Vertex stated on its Q3 earnings call that it has a "clear line of sight to over $100 million in Casgevy revenue this year and significant growth in 2026. If we assume Vertex receives revenues when patients receive their first infusion, and that in 2026, all 300 patients referred to ATCs receive their first infusion, revenues could amount ~$660m.

If we apply a gross to net discount of 30%, we have $462m in revenues, and if we allocate 40% of that to CRISPR Therapeutics, that would represent a potential revenue figure for the company in 2026 of ~$185m. If we then assume a 25% annual revenue growth rate, the figure could rise >$450m by 2030.

That figure may still not be what either Vertex or CRISPR Therapeutics might have hoped for, but it is a significant number, nonetheless, and both companies are working towards easing the preconditioning regime (in order to have bone marrow cells extracted) that patients must undergo, and even creating an in-vivo version. If that happens before 2030, we can multiply the revenue figures quoted above by 5x.

My conclusion is therefore that Casgevy is a slow-burner, but one that, nonetheless, can steadily grow momentum over the years and justify the hype - this is, after all, by far and away the most effective therapy to treat SCD and TDT.

Vertex also notes that in Q3, Casgevy secured reimbursement in Italy, a country with a population of 5k TDT patients, research suggests. In July in the US, 33 states agreed to participate in the US government's Cell and Gene Therapy Access Model, designed to centrally coordinate insurance coverage for such treatments. The momentum is being built, albeit slowly.

Pipeline Progress: Multiple "Moonshots," But Not Yet Time For Lift-Off

In a recent presentation, CRISPR Therapeutics provides the following overview of its product portfolio and pipeline as follows:

This month, management shared some data from its Phase 1 study of CTX-310, indicated for homozygous familial hypercholesterolemia ("HoFH"), severe hypertriglyceridemia ("SHTG"), heterozygous familial hypercholesterolemia ("HeFH"), and mixed dyslipidemias. Data was summarised in a press release as follows:

Phase 1 clinical data for CTX310 demonstrate robust, dose-dependent reductions in circulating ANGPTL3 with a mean reduction from baseline of -73% (maximum -89%), a mean reduction in triglycerides ("TG") of -55% (maximum -84%), and a mean reduction of low-density lipoprotein (LDL) of -49% (maximum -87%) at the highest dose-

-Among participants with elevated baseline TG (>150 mg/dL), a mean reduction of 60% in TG were observed at therapeutic doses-

-CTX310 was well tolerated with no treatment-related serious adverse events and no ≥Grade 3 changes in liver transaminases-

The release also included a quote from Stephen J. Nicholls, lead study investigator and director of the Victorian Heart Institute at Monash University:

Seeing a single-course treatment safely lower both LDL cholesterol and triglycerides is truly unprecedented. If these findings are confirmed in larger studies, a one-time therapy could redefine how we manage lifelong lipid disorders and help prevent cardiovascular disease.

As the study investigator notes, there are more hurdles to overcome - later stage studies with more patients (this Phase 1 study tested CTX-310 in just 15 patients) - but it may be worth noting than in July this year, Eli Lilly (LLY), the world's largest Pharma by market cap, completed the $1.3bn acquisition of Verve Therapeutics, an in-vivo specialist whose lead candidates target the PCSK9 and ANGPTL3 genes.

CRISPR Therapeutics' candidate CTX-320 also targets LPA, an LDL-like lipoprotein synthesized and secreted by hepatocytes, and an independent risk factor for atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease ("ASCVD"), the pharma notes, CTX-340 targets Angiotensinogen ("AGT"), and is being tested as a therapy for hypertension, and CTX-460 targets the SERPINA1 gene that causes alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency ("AATD").

With its CAR-T programs, CRISPR Therapeutics is once again attempting to break new ground in the way patients can be treated. These therapies are allogeneic, as opposed to autologous, i.e., cells are derived from a donor as opposed to the patient themselves, bypassing the need for cell extraction.

Multiple oncology directed autologous therapies have been approved, but no allogeneic ones, due to the difficulty of ensuring patients' immune systems do not reject the donor cells, causing fatal complications.

Can CRISPR Therapeutics' programs change that? I discussed in my last note how its CEO, Samarth Kulkarni, recently highlighted how CTX-112 has shown expansion levels of up to 50,000 copies per microgram, which matches the performance of approved autologous therapies.

The company has promised a "broad" update on CTX-112 by year-end, encompassing both its oncology and autoimmune indications - studies have suggested CAR-T therapies may be pivoted into treating autoimmune conditions - CRISPR Therapeutics could be the first to turn that theory into a commercial reality.

Finally, in diabetes, CRISPR is developing CTX211 and CTX213, the former being in the clinic, with a non-exclusive license with Vertex in place. Again, an update is promised in 2025, and it's worth noting that Sana biotechnology (SANA) stock leaped from ~$1.5 per share, to >$4 per share in January this year after it reported that it had successfully transplanted UP421, its allogeneic primary islet cell therapy engineered with its proprietary hypoimmune ("HIP") technology, into a 42-year-old patient living with type 1 diabetes ("T1D") for over three decades, without the use of any immunosuppression.

CRISPR Therapeutics' says that CTX211 "encapsulates pancreatic progenitor cells derived from pluripotent stem cells with gene-edits for immune evasion and cell survival", and that CTX213 uses "pancreatic progenitor cells derived from edited pluripotent stem cells directly infused vs. delivered via device."

Concluding Analysis: Does CRISPR Therapeutics Still Warrant A Buy Rating?

I have been banging the drum for CRISPR Therapeutics for many years, although I cannot say it has been a great investment for me personally - yet.

On the plus side, shares are up >30% year-to-date, and up 40% on a six-month basis, but on a five-year basis, they are down 48%. That's partly due to an unflattering comparison to a time when the stock was trading at all-time highs, and I remain fairly certain CRISPR Therapeutics is undervalued.

Consider the fact that the company boasts a cash position of ~$1.9bn, then consider that Eli Lilly paid $1.3bn to acquire Verve, and that Sana Biotechnology has a market cap valuation of $1bn. The sum total of these three figures is $4.2bn - CRISPR Therapeutics' market cap valuation is $4.97bn, so ~$800m higher.

How should we value Casgevy? If I am correct in my forecasting that the program could be making a $500m revenue contribution in 2030, and we know that year-to-date, collaboration expense amounted to $160m. Economies of scale dictate this figure may not need to rise dramatically in order to treat more patients, so I'd speculate Casgevy could be earning CRISPR Therapeutics a profit of ~$250m per annum by 2030. How much is a $250m per annum profit on drug sales worth - I'd speculate it adds at least $2.5bn to CRISPR's market cap, based on what a Pharma might pay to acquire such as asset.

We're now close to a $7.5bn valuation - we must acknowledge a serious risk that programs fail - these are "moonshot" programs to a certain extent, and acknowledge the high cash burn, so any CRISPR Therapeutics investors must be prepared to lose money, but what if these moonshot programs succeed?

I have noted several times before how the valuation of rival Intellia Therapeutics (NTLA) soared >$10bn back in 2021 when it shared data showing its in-vivo therapy was able to reduce serum TTR in patients when delivered in vivo. Intellia's share price has suffered in recent months as the company reported a patient death in one of its studies, although its valuation remains >$1bn.

This highlights how carefully CRISPR Therapeutics must tread, and I'd also point to the likes of, e.g., BioNTech (BNTX), whose Car-T programs have promised much and delivered little for many years, and Allogene Therapeutics (ALLO), in the allogeneic space, whose share are down >95% on a five-year basis.

Unfortunately, with CRISPR Therapeutics, there are no guarantees of success - in the clinic, or commercially, and I think it's understandable if investors are somewhat skeptical, and why the share price cannot ever seem to build on momentum.

Nevertheless, thanks to its capable management team, financially prudent and able to succeed where others fail - witness the progress made by this company compared to other CRISPR -focused companies like Editas Medicine (EDIT), or Intellia itself - my mantra has traditionally been "if anyone can, CRISPR Therapeutics can," therefore I am looking forward to the updates to come in 2025 for the CAR-T and diabetes.

From an investment perspective, each time CRISPR Therapeutics' stock stutters and gives up recent gains, I'd call it an opportunity to buy. I prefer to compare the company with two RNA drug developers, Alnylam (ALNY) and Ionis (IONS), whose share prices were stagnant for a decade of more as they wrestled with drug delivery issues, but whose share prices are up >90% year-to-date as they have both secured momentous approvals for drugs targeting heart disease and hereditary angioedema, respectively.

I believe that CRISPR may enjoy similarly successful years towards the end of this decade, as I believe the concept of in-vivo genetic editing is here to stay, and for many reasons - price, patient satisfaction, manufacturing, efficacy, and safety, it could change the face of the pharmaceutical landscape.

I am not completely sold on the CAR-T programs and wonder if shelving them may save the company hundreds of millions in R&D costs, but with its hefty cash position, CRISPR Therapeutics does not to make any knee-jerk decisions - it is in a privileged position, and deservedly so.

This article updates my review published in December 2024 in light of current holdings and recent performance.

SLYV Strategy

SPDR S&P 600 Small Cap Value ETF (SLYV) was launched on 9/25/2000 and tracks the S&P Small Cap 600 Value Index. SLYV has a portfolio of 457 stocks, an expense ratio of 0.15%, and a 30-day SEC yield of 1.97%. Two other ETFs track the same index: Vanguard S&P Small Cap 600 Value ETF (VIOV) and iShares S&P Small-Cap 600 Value ETF (IJS). They are equivalents in theory, but VIOV has a lower expense ratio and IJS higher dollar trading volumes compared to SLYV.

The underlying index blends two of the three factors of the 1992 Fama-French model: value and size. As described by S&P Dow Jones Indices, S&P 600 constituents are classified in value and growth styles based on their valuation ratios and growth metrics. The valuation ratios are book-value-to-price, earnings-to-price, and sales-to-price. By definition, 33% of the parent index exclusively belongs to the value style, 33% to the growth style, and 34% to both styles. The Value style subset defines the S&P Small Cap 600 Value Index. It is reconstituted annually and is capital-weighted, with an adjustment for constituents belonging to both styles.

For instance, a company with a higher value rank than its growth rank is given a higher weight in the Value Index than in the Growth Index. The portfolio turnover rate was 45% in the most recent fiscal year and 58% in the previous year. This article will use as a benchmark the parent index S&P Small Cap 600, represented by iShares Core S&P Small-Cap ETF (IJR).

Portfolio

The portfolio has significant exposure in financials (20.9% of asset value), technology (16.8%), consumer discretionary (14.7%), and industrials (13.9%). Other sectors are below 8%. The sector breakdown is quite close to the benchmark’s, even though SLYV moderately overweights financials, consumer discretionary, materials, and utilities, while it downplays healthcare and industrials.

data.title: Petrobras: Gigantic Yield, It's Time To Be Greedy
data.description:
Article Thesis

Petróleo Brasileiro S.A. - Petrobras (PBR) (PBR.A) reported its most recent quarterly earnings results last week, showing a strong operating performance and good financial results despite a weak oil price environment. The company also announced its dividends for the third quarter, with the yield standing at 11% for PBR and at 12% for PBR.A. The dividend story thus remains intact, even with oil prices (CL1:COM) at a lowish level, which is why I consider Petrobras a good income investment.

Past Coverage

I have written about Petrobras here on Seeking Alpha in the past, with this being my most recent article on the company, in which I gave Petrobras a "Buy" rating. Shares have gained 7% since then, outperforming the broad market's 1% return over the same time frame. With Petrobras releasing its most recent earnings results last week and with the company announcing its dividends for Q3—many investors had worried about the sustainability of PBR's high dividend yield—it's time to update my views on the company today.

Petrobras' Quarterly Performance

Last Friday, Petrobras announced its quarterly earnings results for the third quarter, with the slides being available here. As an E&P-focused energy major, Petrobras, unsurprisingly, is impacted by oil price movements to a large degree. Recently, oil prices have been relatively weak, as we can see in the following chart:

Over the last year, Brent (CO1:COM) prices dropped from around the mid-$70s to the mid-$60s, with two very short spikes to around $80 and two very short periods of oil trading in the $50s. As the above chart shows, Q3 2025 prices were rather weak—weaker than in Q3 2024.

Factors for the rather low oil prices we have seen lately are overproduction worries, subdued economic growth in many markets, especially Europe, and easing tensions in the Middle East. While consumers are, of course, happy about lower oil prices, Petrobras and other oil producers see a negative impact on their revenues and profits. Despite this headwind, however, Petrobras performed quite well during the third quarter, which was largely the result of very strong operational execution:

The company was able to hit a new production record (arguably doing its part in fueling overproduction worries by doing so), with production rising to 3.14 million barrels of oil equivalent per day. That was up by a very strong 17% compared to one year earlier, with production also rising by a pretty sizeable 8% on a sequential basis. Many oil companies, especially the supermajors such as Chevron (CVX), BP (BP), or Exxon Mobil (XOM), are slow-growing; thus, Petrobras' production growth rate in the high-teens range stands out versus its peer group. The strong production growth rate was made possible by production increases in major growth areas for the company, especially in the Buzios ultra-deepwater oil field, where one of Petrobras' FPSOs, Almirante Tamandaré, hit a 225,000 barrel per day level in Q3, with that number rising further in October—Q4 results could thus be pretty good as well, I believe.

Management also stated that increased production from fields that have already been operated for a while was a tailwind -- the company became more efficient and found ways to improve rates at older assets. I like this a lot, as this should be relatively high-margin production that doesn't require a lot of additional capital expenditures.

Thanks to the big production increase during the third quarter, which was largely the result of Petrobras' increased spending on its deepwater assets in recent years, Petrobras was able to (mostly) offset the headwinds from lower oil prices during the quarter. Adjusted EBITDA totaled $12.0 billion during the most recent quarter, which was actually up by a couple of percentage points versus Q3 2024, while adjusted net income declined slightly, from $5.5 billion one year ago to $5.2 billion during this year's third quarter. Considering oil prices, based on a quarterly average, pulled back by close to 15%, pretty stable earnings are a great result, I think. This also positions Petrobras pretty well for an eventual oil price increase—I think that it is unlikely that oil will continue to trade where it trades at right now forever.

Whether due to geopolitical tensions or due to improving economic growth, oil prices should improve at some point, and Petrobras should then see a nice profit increase thanks to its strong production growth and ongoing investments in new production in Buzios, the Foz de Amazonas region, and so on.

Operating cash flows were down compared to one year earlier, but were still pretty strong in absolute terms, at $9.9 billion, or almost $40 billion annualized—for a company that's valued at around $82 billion today, that's very impressive, I believe. Capital expenditures totaled $5.5 billion in Q3, leaving $4.4 billion in free cash flow. While some investors have been worrying that Petrobras would ramp up its investments in potentially unprofitable green energy assets due to political pressures, it remains very focused on its core competence—oil production. 85% of Petrobras' investments in Q3 were in exploration and production, with 11% going to the oil downstream business, i.e., refining and marketing.

At least so far, Petrobras does not spend significant amounts of money on green assets, so these worries seem overblown to me. $4.4 billion in free cash flow for the quarter means an annualized free cash flow yield of a little north of 20%—outstanding relative to other major energy companies, and making for a very low valuation, as Petrobras trades with a sub-5x free cash flow multiple even during a rather weak oil price environment.

Petrobras' Dividend Story Remains Intact

Fears about growing green investments at Petrobras went hand in hand with fears about its dividend, but it looks like those were overblown as well. Petrobras announced its dividends for the third quarter, with a per-share payout of 0.472 Brazilian Real, equal to $0.18.

Since PBR and PBR.A, as they are traded on U.S. stock exchanges, equal two shares of Petrobras, investors will receive $0.36 per share in total for the third quarter, divided into two payments that will be made in February and March. With PBR trading at $13.10 and with PBR.A trading at $12.40, the quarterly yield is 2.7% and 2.9%, respectively—annualized, that's a yield of around 11% for PBR and around 12% for PBR.A.

Even during a phase when Petrobras invests significant sums of money into production growth while oil prices are rather low, the company thus offers a double-digit yield. Despite the high dividend yield, Petrobras' dividend doesn't look very risky—the payout ratio isn't very high, at around 55% to 60%, considering the company's free cash flow yield of around 20%. Petrobras looks like a more appealing dividend pick compared to other oil majors:

The Western supermajors offer yields of 3.3% to 5.6%, which is around one-third of what one can get from Petrobras right now. Petrobras has more Brazil exposure, but I don't think that's a major risk any longer—Lula has now been president for a while, and Petrobras keeps doing well, so worries about left-wing leadership in Brazil may have been overblown.

Takeaway

Petrobras' growth investments are paying off—despite rather weak oil prices during the third quarter, the company saw its profits remain relatively flat, as strong production growth and efficiency gains offset oil price headwinds.

Cash generation remained strong as well, which is why Petrobras decided to offer a nice dividend payment for the third quarter—the forward yield is around 11% to 12%, making Petrobras a very high-yielding income stock. Petrobras has share price upside potential on top of that, thanks to a very inexpensive valuation and profit growth potential, especially if oil prices were to climb from today's subdued level.

data.title: AI, Efficiency, And Dominance: Why Tencent's Best Years May Still Be Ahead
data.description:
At the end of August 2025, I published my last article about Tencent Holdings Limited (OTCPK:TCEHY) and in the conclusion, I wrote:

The times in which Tencent must be seen as a major bargain are certainly over. Since its lows in late 2022, the stock price almost tripled from about HKD 200 to HKD 600. Nevertheless, I don’t see Tencent as expensive or overvalued as the stock is still trading below its intrinsic value and below its previous all-time high of around HKD 770, which might serve as a potential price target in the next few quarters for the stock.

Tencent clearly remains a “Buy” and although we should not expect the stock to triple again over the next two or three years, expecting a return on investment of at least 10% for Tencent seems more than reasonable. Of course, this does not mean we should preclude setbacks and corrections in the years to come. Tencent’s stock price might certainly correct for several months before the stock can move higher.

Since my last article was published, Tencent gained about 10% (in USD), and this is certainly a solid performance over less than three months. Today, on November 13, 2025, Tencent reported its third quarter results, and in the following article, I will argue that Tencent is still a “Buy”. I will argue why I still think that Tencent is an above-average investment and a good pick for long-term investors. Especially the improvements in efficiency due to AI developments must be seen rather bullish, and Tencent is continuing to report double-digit growth rates.

Quarterly Results

We start by looking at the last quarterly results, which were great once again. For starters, revenue increased from RMB 167.2 billion in Q3/24 to RMB 192.9 billion in Q3/25 – resulting in 15.4% year-over-year growth for the top line. Operating income increased 19.2% year-over-year from RMB 53,333 million in the same quarter last year to RMB 63,554 million in this quarter. And diluted earnings per share increased from RMB 5.644 in Q3/24 to RMB 6.779 in Q3/25 – resulting in 20.1% year-over-year bottom-line growth.

Free cash flow, one of the most important metrics for almost every business, was flat and once again RMB 58.5 billion.

Combined MAUs of Weixin and WeChat continued to increase, but with a low pace. Year-over-year, the number grew 2.3% to 1,414 million, and fee-based subscriptions were stable at 265 million (the same as 12 months earlier). Tencent Video had 114 million subscriptions and maintained its lead in the long-form video industry, and Tencent Music had 126 million subscriptions.

When looking at the three different business segments, each one was contributing to growth, but FinTech and Business Services grew “only” 9.6% year-over-year to RMB 58,174 million, and this segment is remaining the weakest segment for Tencent. VAS (Value Added Services) increased revenue 15.9% year-over-year to RMB 95,860 million, and Marketing Services increased revenue 20.8% year-over-year to RMB 36,242 million. These are currently the two segments driving growth.

Revenue was underpinned by ad spend growth in all major categories and impressions, as well as average eCPM increased year-over-year.

Cheap Power And Issuing Bonds

Recently, it was reported that the Chinese government has increased subsidies to cut the energy bills for some of the country’s largest data centers by half. Especially Chinese tech giants – including ByteDance, Alibaba, and Tencent – have been faced with higher electricity costs. Following the trade war and Beijing’s ban of NVIDIA’s artificial intelligence chips, the Chinese government is encouraging the big tech companies to run their data center operations on chips that are produced by local manufacturers in China (including Huawei and Cambricon). Apparently, these chips are much less energy-efficient than NVIDIA’s AI chips, and many firms are obviously struggling with higher energy costs.

I don’t know if this is good news or not. On the one hand, it is certainly good news for technology companies, as the energy bill is reduced and making the companies more profitable (or the companies are able to invest the cash in some other ways). On the other hand, it is also indicating that China is not as efficient in producing AI chips as the United States, and in the global race for artificial intelligence, this could be a disadvantage for Chinese companies like Tencent.

So far, Tencent is not really competing with companies like OpenAI or Alphabet, or Microsoft, but if this should be the case at some point, Chinese companies might be at a disadvantage. Tencent is still generating most of its revenue in Mainland China, and only about 10% of revenue is generated in international markets. International Games generated RMB 20.8 billion in quarterly revenue and are one of the fastest growing segments for Tencent, with 11% quarter-over-quarter and 43% year-over-year growth, and these are business segments where Tencent is a clear market leader and does not compete with the other major AI companies in the United States.

I don’t know how much Tencent is affected by increasing costs. When looking at the third quarter results, we can’t see any hints that expenses are exploding, and margins improved quarter over quarter. Tencent issued new bonds in September 2025, which was the company’s first bond sale in four years. And obviously, Tencent needs additional cash for the intensifying AI race, but U.S. technology giants are issuing new debt as well. And according to Bloomberg, Tencent raised only 9 billion yuan (about $1.3 billion) with interest rates at 2.6% (five-year), 3% (ten-year), and 3.6% (thirty-year). These are rather low-interest rates and indicate the high-quality business Tencent actually is.

AI And Efficiency

While Tencent was also taking on additional debt to finance the AI race, and increasing energy bills are certainly a problem, it also seems like Tencent is getting more efficient when looking at margins. Year-over-year, gross margin improved from 53% to 56% and operating margin improved from 31.9% in Q3/24 to 33.0% in Q3/25.

And during a recent conference, Tencent indicated that the implementation of an in-house AI-powered platform called WeDev led to a 67% increase in the company’s R&D automation rate. Additionally, more than a quarter of code-related problems are now discovered by AI, which is leading to a reduction of eight hours for fixing each bug.

While we don’t know how much additional revenue Tencent might generate by AI-powered tools and products, these numbers are indicating that Tencent might be able to lower its expenses over time as a sizeable portion of coding tasks might be fulfilled by AI in the future. Tencent also reported that half of all new code written so far in 2025 has been done with the help of AI, which was leading to a 40% drop in the average amount of time that was spent on coding tasks.

But Tencent is also offering its language models and AI-tools as open source (mostly the language models) and as SaaS (several AI tools). In a press release, Tencent wrote:

The upgraded SaaS+AI toolkit enhances office collaboration, including AI Minutes in Tencent Meetings, which has seen a year-on-year growth rate of 150% over the past year. It also supports knowledge management, such as Tencent LearnShare, currently used by over 300,000 enterprises enjoying 92% response accuracy. CodeBuddy, an AI-powered AI coding tool for developers, reduces coding time by 40% and increases R&D efficiency by 16%.

Up until now, Tencent is not seen as one of the major cloud service providers. Nevertheless, more than 90% of leading Chinese internet companies, and 95% of leading Chinese gaming companies, are using Tencent Cloud to support global expansion initiatives. Of course, it remains unclear which companies qualify as “leading”, but we can be optimistic that Tencent is slowly developing into a more important player in the cloud market as well.

Growth

Summing up the growth expectations, we can be quite optimistic that Tencent will continue its double-digit growth path in the years to come. In the case of Tencent, improving margins will contribute to growth in the coming years, as will share buybacks. However, the biggest contributor to overall growth will be top-line growth.

Analysts are expecting revenue to grow with a CAGR of 10.53% in the years between fiscal 2024 and fiscal 2034, which seems reasonable when looking at the growth rates Tencent was reporting not only in the last few quarters but also over the long run.

And these growth rates for revenue might seem reasonable. When combining top-line growth of 10% to 11% with slightly improving margins and share buybacks, Tencent should be able to grow its bottom line at least 12% annually for the years to come.

Intrinsic Value Calculation

A final step in every analysis is to determine whether a stock is undervalued and still a good investment, or if a great business might be overvalued already. When looking at the simple valuation multiples, we can’t make the argument anymore that Tencent is a bargain. At the time of writing, Tencent is trading for 27.2 times earnings. This is still below the long-term average of 33.88, nevertheless, a valuation multiple close to 30 is certainly not indicating a bargain anymore, and a company has to grow with a high pace to justify these valuation multiples.

Instead of the price-earnings ratio, we can look at the price-to-free-cash-flow ratio, which is currently at 19.7. And for a business growing with a high pace, a valuation multiple around 20 can certainly be seen as reasonable and might indicate a stock that is still undervalued. The P/FCF ratio was lower than the P/E ratio most of the time in the last almost 20 years, but I would usually see the P/FCF ratio as the better ratio among the two.

The assessment that Tencent is probably still undervalued can be backed up by using a discount cash flow calculation. As always, we calculate with a 10% discount rate (as this is the annual return we like to achieve at least from our investment). Additionally, we calculate with the last reported number of diluted shares outstanding (9,232 million in Q3/25).

The trailing twelve months free cash flow was almost the same as in the previous quarter (RMB 153 billion), and we will use that number as the basis once again. And similar to previous articles, we calculate with a growth rate of 12% once again, followed by 6% growth till perpetuity. Calculating with these assumptions leads to an intrinsic value of RMB 633 and HKD 692. At the time of writing, Tencent is trading for HKD 656, and the stock is therefore still slightly undervalued.

Additionally, we can make the argument that the free cash flow we are taking as basis is rather pessimistic, as the number was stagnating or even slightly declining in the last few quarters. We can therefore make the argument for an improving free cash flow conversion, and free cash flow might be rather RMB 175 billion to RMB 200 billion next year. And when using RMB 175 billion as basis, we get an intrinsic value of HKD 791, and Tencent would therefore be trading about 20% below its intrinsic value.

Conclusion

I remain bullish about Tencent in the years to come and confident that the business will continue growing at a high rate (most likely in the double-digits), and investors should be able to achieve at least 10% annual return from an investment.

Tencent is not a major bargain anymore, and compared to other Chinese technology companies like Alibaba or Baidu, it is trading for higher valuation multiples. But Tencent continues to perform with high levels of consistency and is one of the better long-term investments in my opinion. Especially, the high levels of stability and consistency for the business are speaking for Tencent as a long-term investment.

And while it remains unclear if Tencent can actually generate huge amounts of revenue from AI tools in the years to come, it seems like the company found a way to use AI to reduce coding time and therefore expenses, which might have a positive impact on margins in the years to come.
