data.title: Sealed Air Corporation Pops On Takeover Rumors, Shares Can Still Climb Higher
data.description:
November 13 was a fascinating day for investors in Sealed Air Corporation (SEE). The company, which produces Bubble Wrap, as well as other packaging materials, saw its share price jump around 19.4% after news broke, the day prior, that it is engaging in talks with a private equity firm by the name of Clayton Dubilier & Rice to potentially be taken private. I have been saying for a while now that the stock is meaningfully undervalued. Despite some troubles that the company has experienced from a revenue perspective in recent years, and even though some profitability metrics have also worsened, this is a very cheap and high-quality player. In fact, in my last article about it, published in early October of this year, I argued that other investors were finally starting to see what I had been seeing.

Since then, shares are up 23.5%, which is massively above the 0.5% increase that the S&P 500 has seen over the same period. And since my article before that, published in June of this year, shares are now up 41.3% compared to the 13.4% rise that the market has enjoyed. Although the easy money has probably been had if these rumors turn out to be true, I would argue that further upside is probable. The firm's overall net debt picture makes it a relatively lower-risk prospect. Management has been working hard to cut costs, with the target of capturing annualized savings of between $140 million and $160 million. Those efforts are finally starting to show on the firm's bottom line. And when you add on top of it how shares are priced even after rising so much, I think that this is a very compelling "Buy" candidate.

A turnaround play

I would argue that it would be appropriate to describe Sealed Air Corporation as something of a turnaround play. In the chart above, you can see financial results for the 2022 through 2024 fiscal years. Revenue for the company contracted during this time from $5.64 billion to $5.39 billion. This drop was driven entirely by the Protective segment, which, as its name suggests, focuses on the production and sale of protective packaging products such as its signature Bubble Wrap brand, as well as others. It also has its own Autobag brand bagging systems, certain types of suspension and retention packaging products, and more.

During these last three years, revenue contracted from $2.32 billion to $1.81 billion. This decline was driven by both price drops and lower volume, with management blaming this on a combination of factors. Volumes were the biggest problem, as shown in the chart below. Management said that this was the result of continued market pressures and destocking in the industrial and fulfillment sectors. The price declines, meanwhile, were primarily because of pricing pressures from competitors and the ability of the company to pass on lower raw material costs to its customers in the form of savings. Naturally, this decline in revenue brought with it a decline in profitability for the segment. But the good news is that this weakness was offset to some extent by a strong Food segment.

As its name suggests, the Food segment focuses on selling food related packaging products. From 2022 through 2024, revenue actually jumped from $3.32 billion to $3.58 billion. Overall pricing during this time was virtually flat, though there was a jump in volumes of $136.5 million from 2023 to 2024 that management said could be chalked up to higher-end market demand and competitive share gains in its space. But the big driver, beyond any doubt, was the firm's purchase of Liquibox, which was acquired in 2023. That enterprise added $285 million in revenue that year and another $23.5 million in revenue in 2024.

This year, things have started to pay off. Even though both operating segments of the company experienced weakness, with the Food segment reporting a $2.3 million decline in revenue thanks to lower volumes of $12 million and foreign currency fluctuations of $11.6 million that were offset mostly by a $21.3 million benefit from higher pricing, and even with the Protective segment dropping from $1.36 billion to $1.30 billion as lower pricing and volume from continued end market weakness negatively affected results, overall profitability for the company improved.

In fact, even though revenue declined from $4.02 billion to $3.96 billion for the first nine months of 2025 compared to the same time last year, net income jumped from $269.9 million to $396.8 million. Even on an adjusted basis, we got an increase from $349.2 million to $379.7 million. The improvements that the company saw here were driven in part by lower selling, general, and administrative costs. On top of this, interest expense for the company declined as management focused on meaningful debt reduction.

The improvements on the operational side can be chalked up in large part to the firm’s CTO2Grow program, which is a major cost-cutting initiative aimed at reducing expenses by between $140 million and $160 million. This is a process that has already been mostly paid for by the company. And as for the debt reduction, as of the most recent quarter, the company had net debt of $3.89 billion. That compares to the $4.07 billion that it had at the end of last year. That's a total debt reduction of $177.9 million. And in all likelihood, more is expected by the end of this year, seeing as how management has targeted $250 million in debt pay down this year in its entirety.

Although the Protective segment for the company continues to see bottom-line pain, with gross profit declining from $408.7 million to $376.1 million year over year, this was more than offset by the Food segment, which saw a pop from $810.1 million to $824.7 million. And the interest expense reduction was even more impactful in terms of bottom line results, with the decline year over year from $188.9 million to $168 million.

Management recently increased guidance for this year. Previously, they anticipated revenue of between $5.1 billion and $5.5 billion. The midpoint of that is still the same at $5.3 billion, but the lower end of the range has been pushed up to $5.275 billion, while the higher end of the range has been pushed down to $5.325 billion. However, EBITDA for the company is now expected to be between $1.12 billion and $1.14 billion.

That compares to the $1.075 billion to $1.175 billion previously anticipated. The firm was also thinking before that adjusted earnings per share would be between $2.90 and $3.30. But now, that range has been pushed up to between $3.25 and $3.35. At the midpoint, that would mean an adjusted net income of $485.8 million. And in all likelihood, we would be looking at adjusted operating cash flow of around $899.1 million.

In the chart below, you can see what all of this means for the valuation of the company. Focusing on the cash flow metrics, we can see that even after the share price jumped, the business is trading in the high single-digit range. To put this in perspective, in the same chart, I stacked Sealed Air Corporation up against five other similar firms. And even after seeing its share price rise so much, it is still around the middle of the group, with two of the five firms trading cheaper than it is.

Another positive here is that, even if net debt does not decline from this point on, the firm's leverage is certainly under control. In the chart below, you can see these companies compared to our prospect on a net leverage basis. With a net leverage ratio of 3.44, we can see that three of the five enterprises have higher leverage than what Sealed Air Corporation currently has at this time. And if management reduces debt even more, hitting its target for the end of this year, we would be looking at a net leverage ratio of about 3.38.

Takeaway

Given this data, I must say that I am not surprised to see a private equity firm interested in Sealed Air Corporation. A lot of the hard work has already been done by management to improve costs. At some point, the weakness it is seeing will subside. And when that does, that will make things even more attractive. Given how cheap shares are, even if a deal does not materialize, I would say that Sealed Air Corporation is worth considering. And I would not be shocked at all if any buyout is done at a materially higher price than what the firm is currently trading for.

data.title: Day One Biopharmaceuticals: Mersana Acquisition Ignites March Towards Unmet Need
data.description:
The last time I spoke about Day One Biopharmaceuticals, Inc. (DAWN), it was in a Seeking Alpha article entitled "Day One Biopharmaceuticals: NDA Filing Decision Is An Inflection Point To Watch." With respect to this article, I noted the opportunity that this company had whereby its drug tovorafenib was to be reviewed for potential U.S. marketing approval as a kinase inhibitor for the treatment of patients ages 6 months and older with relapsed/refractory pediatric low-grade glioma (pLGG) who harbor the BRAF fusions or rearrangement, or BRAF V600 mutation.

As an update, I'm happy to report that since then, Day One has been able to obtain FDA approval of this drug, now marketed as OJEMDA, to treat these patients with this type of pediatric brain cancer. This company is doing very well at the moment because, since then, it has been able to continue to grow its revenues for this drug. Consider that in the most recently reported Q3 of 2025, it noted that it generated OJEMDA net product revenue of $38.5 million. This was a quarter-over-quarter growth percentage increase of 15%.

Secondly, this company is already working its way towards expanding the possible patient population it can go after in terms of pLGG. How so? Well, the reason why is because it is in the middle of evaluating OJEMDA monotherapy in the phase 3 FIREFLY-2, treating patients with RAF mutation (altered) pLGG who required front-line systemic therapy. The point of this program is that the company is on track to complete enrollment from this FIREFLY-2 study in the 1st half of 2026. The only downside with this program is that the company has marketing rights for this program in the United States, but every other territory outside of this has rights given to Ipsen.

I had a Strong Buy rating when I last wrote about this, and I believe it is important to keep this rating. The reason why is because of the huge news of the day, which is that Day One is paying $25 per share of cash to acquire Mersana Therapeutics (MRSN).

This is only the initial part of the deal, as this transaction also includes the addition of a contingent value right (CVR) of up to $30.25. If everything goes well with both parts of the deal, then the total equity value of the agreement could reach $55.25 per share in cash. The reason for this merger agreement was so that Day One could get its hands on Emi-Le (XMT-1660), an antibody-drug conjugate (ADC) directed against B7-H4-expressing solid tumors. With the first program already explored in a phase 1 study, using this ADC to target patients with adenoid cystic carcinoma type-1 (ACC-1).

With sales of OJEMDA growing well, along with a possible expansion opportunity towards 1st-line RAF-altered pLGG, plus the acquisition agreement of Mersana Therapeutics to bring on board Emi-Le as a differentiated B7-H4 ADC, I believe that I should keep the Strong Buy rating on Mersana Therapeutics.

Acquisition Of Emi-Le Makes Sense From A Strategic Standpoint

As I noted above, Day One Biopharmaceuticals announced that it had entered into a merger agreement to acquire Mersana Therapeutics. Initially, the deal is to be valued at $25 per share at closing, which represents an equity market value of $129 million. Of course, this then includes the contingent value right (CVR) of $30.25 in cash that has been set forth as well. Again, if all of the expected milestones for the entire CVR go through, then this would value the total deal at $285 million (total $55.25 per share in cash). The truth is that the CVR is split up a lot, and thus, this CVR is not going to happen immediately. It is going to be split according to clinical milestones and then regulatory/sales-based milestones. With that being said, the intended clinical milestones in place are as follows:

  • Development milestone of existing partnership agreement - eligible to earn CVR of $1.25 per share.
  • Breakthrough Therapy Designation granted for the ACC indication by FDA - eligible to earn CVR of $1.00 per share.
  • Dosing of first patient in a registrational trial using Emi-Le for the treatment of patients with ACC-1 - eligible to earn $4.00 per share.

Then, there is the other aspect to consider, which is regulatory/sales-based milestones. There are a total of 6 other CVRS to be earned with this category. However, the largest one to mention would be the regulatory approval of Emi-Le for the treatment of patients with ACC-1 by the FDA. If this happens, then the CVR to be earned for this would be $9.00 per share. The last item to mention relating to this deal is that, of course, it has to make it through rigorous regulatory scrutiny before this deal can be finalized. If all goes well in terms of closing conditions, then it is expected to close by the end of January 2026.

The thing is that the ability to acquire Mersana Therapeutics makes sense from two strategic vantage points. The first vantage point is that Day One's pipeline is built on targeting cancers with an unmet medical need. With this merger deal in place, it obtains Emi-Le, which is a differentiated antibody-drug conjugate (ADC) that targets B7-H4 solid tumors. One of the first programs that Mersana was working on, before it was acquired, was the development of Emi-Le to treat patients with adenoid cystic carcinoma type-1 (ACC-1).

What made this a strong program, and likely why Day One chose to throw the development of Emi-Le into its pipeline, is that there was proof-of-concept established in a phase 1 study. That is, there was antitumor activity noted in an early-stage study using this particular drug to treat these patients. There was first the release of positive data in January 2025, but then the company provided an update of additional results that were released as an oral presentation at the American Society of Clinical Oncology (ASCO) 2025 program. This was ideal data because it was noted that there was a 31% (8 out of 26) confirmed objective response rate (ORR) across all solid tumor types of B7-H4 expression.

The point here is that the goal of this company is to shift a focus towards solid tumors that have high expression of this protein. This is a biomarker that can be used to recruit patients who are likely to respond to treatment with Emi-Le. For instance, this specific group of patients who obtained a confirmed ORR of 31% had a B7-H4 tumor proportion score of 70% or higher. This alone is good enough, but what is even better is that the ORR percentage went up as the prior lines of therapies these patients took increased. When looking at patients who received ≤4 prior lines of therapy, their ORR increased to 44%.

What I went over above was just how the data was viewed in a positive manner. Another aspect to consider is the unmet medical need for patients with adenoid cystic carcinoma type-1 (ACC-1). Consider that at the present time, there are no FDA-approved therapies for these patients. In addition, there is no clearly defined standard of care (SOC) therapy for them either.

To this end, the belief by Day One is that it could shift towards a potential registration study immediately because of this. That is, there might be a quick registration path available for it to take in the development of this drug targeting these ACC-1 patients. If it can gain adequate guidance from the FDA, then Emi-Le might have a quick pathway towards U.S. marketing approval for these patients.

It is my opinion that such an acquisition likely wasn't done on the premise of obtaining an ADC for only one indication of ACC-1. What I mean by this is that there are several other solid tumors that have high expression of B7-H4. If you look at the link above with the data Mersana released with the 31% ORR number, it not only saw a positive signal in targeting patients with ACC-1, but also in those who had triple-negative breast cancer (TNBC). In terms of TNBC, it was stated that there were positive signals in these patients who had already been treated with a topoisomerase-1 inhibitor. The point is that B7-H4 is highly expressed in many solid tumor types but is limited in healthy tissue. This is what makes the development of this ADC an ideal one.

The other strategic reason why it made sense for Day One to acquire Mersana Therapeutics for Emi-Le was because it already had another ADC in place. This is an ADC directed to target PTK7-expressing solid tumors known as DAY301. Specifically, the company is in the process of running a phase 1a/1b open-label, dose-escalation study using this drug to target patients with locally advanced or metastatic solid tumors. The premise for advancing this is because of preclinical studies going well to target such patients with advanced solid tumors.

Speaking of which, PTK7 targeting is ideal because there are many tumor types that highly express this protein on their cell surface, and they are as follows:

  • Triple-negative breast cancer (TNBC) which I mentioned above.
  • Non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC).
  • Platinum-resistant ovarian cancer (PROC).
  • Osteosarcoma.
  • Neuroblastoma.

The point of this ADC is not just to be a similar drug like others. The goal was to be developed to overcome limitations of other ADCs targeting PTK7. In essence, to potentially become the best-in-class ADC targeting this protein. It is important to state, though, that this candidate wasn't developed by Day One. This is a drug it had licensed from MabCare Therapeutics. Even though this drug is licensed from this entity in China, Day One was able to obtain worldwide exclusive rights to DAY301. The only exclusion is that it holds no rights for this drug in the Greater China region.

Financials

According to the 10-Q SEC Filing, Day One Biopharmaceuticals had cash, cash equivalents, and short-term investments of $451.6 million as of September 30, 2025. The company had to use its strong cash position in order to acquire Mersana Therapeutics. The company noted that it should have some cash to fund its operations, but the 10-Q SEC Filing before this acquisition notes a risk of needing to raise additional cash. The reason why is because, according to this SEC filing, there was an estimation made that the company of its cash runway that it would only have enough cash on hand to fund its operations at least through the next 12 months.

This means it is my belief at some point in 2026 that it may need to enact some type of cash-raising activity to keep its operations going. With that being said, there is an ATM agreement in place that it could utilize. This is an Equity Distribution Agreement, it had entered into with Piper Sandler & Co. and JonesTrading Institutional Services LLC as sales agents. This was done so that shares of common stock could be issued and sold with an aggregate offering price of $250 million. The good news to note about this is that as of September 30, 2025, no shares have been sold under this agreement. This biotech is different than most others for the simple reason that it is already generating revenues with an approved drug.

This drug, known as OJEMDA has already been approved by the FDA to treat patients 6 months or older with relapse/refractory pediatric low-grade glioma (pLGG) who harbor a BRAF fusion or rearrangement or a BRAF V600 mutation. Sales of OJEMDA in Q3 of 2025 reached $38.5 million, which was a growth percentage of 15% from the prior Q2 of 2025.

The company burns roughly $59.5 million of cash per quarter. This is broken down into $31.4 million in R&D expenses and then $28.1 million in SG&A expenses.

Risks To Business

There are several risks that investors should be aware of before investing in Day One Biopharmaceuticals. The first risk to consider would be in terms of this latest merger agreement with Mersana Therapeutics. The risk here is that even though there has been sufficient antitumor activity observed in a phase 1 study using Emi-Le to treat these patients with adenoid cystic carcinoma type-1 (ACC-1), there is no assurance that this will be observed in other studies. The second risk to consider here would be in terms of the belief by the company that the unmet medical need of this indication (no FDA-approved therapies) could mean a quicker registration pathway for marketing approval. The risk here is that there is no assurance that the FDA is going to allow a quicker pathway route for FDA approval of Emi-Le for the treatment of these patients with ACC-1.

The second risk to consider would be in terms of its net product revenues of OJEMDA, which I went over above. As I stated, quarter-over-quarter growth for net product revenues of this drug was 15% to $38.5 million. The risk here is that despite achieving this sales outcome, and the company raising its full-year 2025 revenue guidance for OJEMDA, there is no guarantee that its sales expectations over the next several quarters are going to be met.

The third risk to consider would be in terms of DAY301 as a PKT7-targeted ADC being developed to treat patients with solid tumors. The risk here is that the concept of mode of action of this ADC targeting tumors has only been established in preclinical testing. This means there is no assurance that the data that has been generated in preclinical testing is going to be shown again in phase 1 human testing. The hope is that it can advance this candidate because it worked hard to obtain worldwide exclusive rights for DAY301, targeting solid tumors that express PKT7.

The fourth risk to consider here would be in terms of the merger agreement deal that Day One Biopharmaceuticals made with Mersana Therapeutics. The reason why is because this particular deal, to acquire this company and Emi-Le is still contingent upon customary closing conditions and other regulatory laws. If all goes well, the transaction is expected to close at the end of January 2026.

The first risk here is that there is no assurance that this deal will be able to get through all satisfactory closing conditions without a problem. A second risk to consider would be in terms of the CVR of $30.25 per share in cash. The reason why is because this portion of the deal is tied to several separate milestones that must be met. Thus, the risk here is that there is no guarantee that the CVR laid out will be partially or fully attained over time. A lot of the milestones might be rough to achieve. For instance, the CVR of $9.00 per share tied to Emi-Le FDA approval is not a guaranteed outcome. If a rejection of approval happens, then shareholders would not be entitled to receive that specific CVR.

The fifth and final risk to consider would be in terms of the financial position that this company finds itself in. In the press release, it notes that its vigorous cash position allowed it to acquire Mersana Therapeutics as part of the initial $25 per share amount. However, in the 10-Q SEC Filing it released on November 4, 2025, it notes in there that it believes that its cash on hand will only be enough to fund its operations at least through the next 12 months. This means it will either have to find a way to raise funds through a financial transaction in 2026, or it may have to tap into the ATM agreement I noted above that it had made with Piper Sandler & Co. and JonesTrading Institutional Services LLC as sales agents.

Conclusion

I believe that Day One Biopharmaceuticals made the right move to acquire Mersana. The reasons why it was a good idea, I have gone over above. For starters, it brings in an ADC that targets B7-H4, which is expressed in many solid tumors but not expressed much, if at all, in healthy tissues. This would be Emi-Le, which has already shown antitumor activity in treating patients with adenoid cystic carcinoma type-1 (ACC-1). The premise for this program is that there are no FDA-approved therapies for ACC-1; thus, this is a good target indication to go after because of that. Plus, there might be an expedited registrational pathway of FDA approval as well. Of course, that's pending there are positive discussions with the U.S. FDA for it to obtain such an accelerated approval pathway.

The beauty of Day One obtaining Emi-Le through the Mersana Therapeutics acquisition is that it is not just to be applied to the targeting of patients with ACC-1. It is that it can be utilized for a variety of other cancers. Thus, the acquisition makes sense from a strategic point of potentially obtaining approval for an indication that is considered to be an unmet medical need. Plus, the fact that it already had another ADC in its pipeline being developed by the company as well, known as DAY301. With everything mentioned above, plus the potential to obtain Emi-Le as an ADC targeting B7-H4-expressing solid tumors, I believe that I should keep the Strong Buy rating on this stock.

data.title: Wall Street Lunch: Michael Burry Waves Bye
data.description:
Listen below or on the go on Apple Podcasts and Spotify

Burry wound up his fund just days after disclosing bets against AI trade. (0:15). Disney tumbles on TV, film weakness. (1:55) AI trade could kill buybacks. (3:39)

This is an abridged transcript of the podcast:

Our top story so far, from Big Short to Big Mystery.

Michael Burry has wound up his hedge fund, Scion Asset Management, just days after making waves by betting against the AI trade.

Scion terminated its registration with the SEC effective November 10. In a letter to investors dated October 27 and confirmed by the Financial Times, Burry wrote, “With a heavy heart, I will liquidate the funds and return capital—minus a small audit and tax holdback—by year’s end. My estimation of value in securities is not now, and has not been for some time, in sync with the markets.”

He added that investors could reach out to associate PM Phil Clifton about his next move. But on X, Burry teased “much better things November 25.”

Last week, Scion disclosed bearish positions in Nvidia (NVDA) and Palantir (PLTR). The man who famously predicted the 2008 housing crisis has recently been warning about inflated AI valuations and creative accounting at hyperscalers. He argues that cloud and AI infrastructure firms are boosting earnings by extending the “useful life” of their assets—what he calls one of the most common modern accounting tricks.

Burry estimates the practice could understate depreciation by $176 billion between 2026 and 2028, overstating profits at Oracle (ORCL) and Meta (META) by more than 20%.

You can check out a full timeline of Burry’s Wall Street arc on Seeking Alpha — where, by the way, we’re having a Black Friday sale with 20% off all products.

In today’s trading, investors are echoing Burry’s pessimism about tech – for this session at least.

The Nasdaq (COMP.IND) is the weakest among the major averages, down 2% in afternoon trading. The S&P (SP500) and Dow (DJI) are off more than 1%.

Some of the biggest decliners include Super Micro Computer (SMCI), Tesla (TSLA) and Palantir (PLTR).

Among active stocks, Disney (DIS) is tumbling on weakness in its traditional TV and film businesses in fiscal Q4 – overshadowing growth in streaming and parks.

Bank of America’s Jessica Reif Ehrlich says Disney’s results are likely to lean heavily on the back half of the year.

The first quarter, she says, will be weighed down by about $150 million in pre-opening and dry-dock costs in the Experiences business, a $400 million hit from tough theatrical comparisons, $140 million in lower political ad revenue, and a $73 million drag from Star India.

On the bright side, Disney announced a 50% boost to its dividend and plans to double share buybacks to $7 billion in fiscal 2026.

Verizon (VZ) — under new CEO leadership promising cost cuts and a turnaround — plans to lay off roughly 15,000 workers and transition about 200 stores into franchises.

The telecom has lost postpaid phone subscribers for three straight quarters as competition intensifies in both home internet and mobile service.

And Firefly Aerospace (FLY) is soaring after a strong earnings report and a Deutsche Bank upgrade to Buy.

Analyst Edison Yu says a fix for the Alpha rocket’s first-stage engine issue gives investors better visibility into Flight #7 — expected late this year or early 2026.

In other news of note, Target (TGT) is hoping a new staff policy will bring shoppers closer — literally.

USA Today says the policy - called the 10-4 program - requires employees within 10 feet of shoppers to smile, make eye contact and wave. If shoppers come closer, within 4 feet, employees must greet them and initiate a warm helpful interaction.

Chief Stores Officer Adrienne Costanzo says: "Heading into the holiday, we're making adjustments and implementing new ways to increase connection during the most important time of the year."

And in the Wall Street Research Corner, the race for AI dominance could soon wipe out one of corporate America's favorite avenues for shareholder return: the stock buyback.

The market may be on the brink of a “de facto Fed tightening,” according to Nomura strategist Charlie McElligott. If growth and inflation reaccelerate — a scenario he says would catch most investors flat-footed — then loose financial conditions could create “such a profound wealth effect via the stock market” that traders would have to price in a less dovish Fed path.

“In that kind of backdrop,” he says, the AI theme — which is the stock market — begins to lean on credit spreads to keep chasing capex, while free-cash-flow burn hits the cash that’s funded buybacks for the past 15 years.

In other words, hello data centers, bye bye buybacks.
